exercises.name: KB 2 Hand Curtsy Lunge to Lateral Lunge
DB Plank Push Up w/ Lateral Shift
KB Lateral Shift w/ Hand Switch
DB 1arm Snatch w/ Foot on Bench
DB Bridge and Press on bench
DB Offset Front Rack Lateral Lunge

days.cardio_note: The 2:00 Moderate builds your base and elevates heart rate before the distance challenge. Push the .3 mi at true Hard effort — your time is determined by how hard you work.
Two rounds of 2:00 Moderate into 2:00 Hard build sustained cardiovascular endurance. The final 1:00 Moderate brings your heart rate down so you're recovered and ready for the next station.

exercises.name: DB Neutral Grip Row on Bench
Ball Overhead Slam to Squat Jump

days.cardio_note: Short Max bursts spike the heart rate higher than sustained hard efforts can, increasing cardiovascular adaptation over time. The 1:00 Easy keeps blood moving so members recover fully and can hit true Max each round.

exercises.name: Ball Reverse Lunge w/ rotation alt.

days.cardio_note: The 1:00 Easy primes the legs and elevates heart rate so members can attack the calorie target at true Hard effort. Calories measure work output — the harder they push, the faster they're done.

exercises.name: DB Chest Press and Chest Fly alt.

days.cardio_note: Two full minutes at Hard builds real aerobic capacity by forcing the body to sustain elevated output. The final 1:00 Moderate brings heart rate down so members arrive at the next station fresh and ready to go.

Keeping the floor at Moderate means the cardiovascular system stays loaded for the entire 9:00 — there is no true rest, just a shift in intensity. Sustained elevation at this level builds aerobic endurance efficiently and keeps the heart rate working the whole time.
Unlike timed intervals, distance targets are earned — the effort put in directly determines the outcome. Use the 1:00 Easy to settle in before committing fully to the .3 mi Hard.

exercises.name: DB SL Hip Thrust on Bench

days.cardio_note: Each Hard interval is longer than the last, progressively challenging the cardiovascular system as fatigue builds. The Moderate recoveries stay consistent, giving just enough reset to hit the next Hard effort with full commitment.

The pyramid structure peaks at Max before descending back through every zone, giving members a complete cardiovascular experience. Starting and ending in Easy ensures full recovery before the next station.
The :40 Easy keeps legs moving so the body stays primed to surge into the 2:00 Hard. The final 1:00 Moderate is a controlled descent, leaving members recovered and ready at the next station.

Brief Max bursts followed by active Easy recovery train the body to spike and recover repeatedly, building cardiovascular efficiency. The final 1:00 Moderate closes the station with a controlled descent.

warmup: 06/12/2026 - Complete the following stations for 9 mins each,

exercises.name: KB Farmer Carry Knee Raise in Place

days.cardio_note: Each Hard interval is shorter than the last, so members can push harder as the rounds progress — the end is always closer than the last. Consistent 1:00 Moderate recoveries keep the pattern predictable so members can stay focused on the work.

The 2:00 Moderate builds momentum so members arrive at the distance target with an elevated heart rate and legs already working. On the .3 mi, effort determines the outcome — push harder, finish faster.

exercises.name: Plank w/ knee to both elbows

days.cardio_note: Each round builds from Moderate into a 2:00 Hard effort, with a brief Easy to reset before going again. The final 1:00 Moderate is a controlled close, leaving members recovered and ready for the next station.
Equal intervals across all three zones keep the pattern simple and predictable, letting members focus entirely on hitting each intensity. The goal is the same effort on round four as round one.

exercises.name: DB Offset Weight Bulgarian Split Squat
KB Lateral Shift w/ Hand Switch

days.cardio_note: Alternating Moderate and Hard intervals challenge members to recover just enough before pushing back into high intensity, driving cardiovascular adaptation through repeated effort.

exercises.name: BW Reverse Lunge w/ Reach Alt.

days.floor_note: Benchmark Day · 3 rounds. Round 1 is a lighter warm-up; round 2 sets your working weight; round 3 is the round that counts — log reps for each main lift.

exercises.name: DB Rev. Lunge w/ Rotation Alt.

days.cardio_note: Members push through a calorie target at Hard intensity, driving strong consistent effort to hit the goal quickly before recovering in the Easy interval.

exercises.name: DB Rev. Lunge to Step Up Alt.

days.cardio_note: Members build into Hard effort with each distance marker, using the Moderate interval to recover just enough before pushing back into high intensity.

exercises.name: BW Reverse Lunge w/ Reach Alt.

days.floor_note: Benchmark Day · 3 rounds. Round 1 is a lighter warm-up; round 2 sets your working weight; round 3 is the round that counts — log reps for each main lift.

days.cardio_note: Members build through progressively longer Hard intervals, with Moderate recovery between each push. The pyramid structure peaks at the center before stepping back down, challenging members to maintain effort as fatigue accumulates.

exercises.name: DB Bent Over Row Knee on Bench

days.cardio_note: Coach members to treat each Max burst like a race — everything they have for :30, then back off and breathe. The Easy minute is the reset, not the reward. Each round they should feel like they can go again.

exercises.name: DB Pullover to Chest Press to Sit Up on bench

days.cardio_note: The Easy interval allows heart rate to drop before members drive back into Hard effort — having a distance target to chase pushes strong output.
Coach members to treat each round as a build — pushing through the final Hard interval under accumulated fatigue is what drives cardiovascular adaptation.

exercises.name: DB Incline Chest Press x2reps alt.

days.cardio_note: Members build from a longer Moderate distance into a shorter Hard push — the reduced target at Hard intensity makes the effort feel achievable while still driving cardiovascular output.
Coach members to use the Moderate interval to build into the effort — arriving at Hard already elevated means they can hit it hard and recover fully in the Easy before going again.
Coach members to use the Moderate to climb through Hard and arrive at Max already loaded — the :30 is short enough to give everything they have before resetting in the Easy.
Short Hard bursts followed by longer Moderate recovery bring heart rate down enough that members can sustain quality effort through all four rounds.
Coach members to bank their energy in the Easy interval — the calorie target at Hard is the work, so they should arrive ready to attack it.
Push members to maintain effort through the Moderate so heart rate is already climbing when they hit Max — that's what turns a short burst into a true max effort.
:30 sprints past current threshold will increase aerobic capacity over time.  1:30 total recovery allows consistency in Max efforts.
Moderate is a pace members could hold for many minutes — hard is where it starts to feel unsustainable. Repeating that shift trains the body to recover faster and sustain higher efforts over time.
The moderate-to-hard progression gradually builds fatigue across three rounds. The final max effort under fatigue forces the body to recruit fast-twitch muscle fibers, improving power output.
The longer moderate intervals keep members right at the edge of sustainable effort before the hard pushes past it. Repeating this twice drives adaptation without burning members out.

The goal is to hit 10 calories as fast as possible each round, pushing past the sustainable effort threshold. The 1:00 recovery resets members enough to maintain that intensity across every hard interval.

exercises.name: Plank with Knee to both elbows
Ball Push Up Hands on Ball

days.cardio_note: Each round builds to a challenging Hard effort before coming back down — teaching the body to flush lactate and recover while still moving.

This ascending ladder repeats three times, training the body to approach and recover from its lactate threshold each round. That repeated stress and recovery is what drives aerobic adaptation and improves endurance over time.

Pairing fixed rest with a distance target encourages honest, consistent effort rather than pacing to a clock. Repeatedly hitting .2 miles at hard effort taxes the cardiovascular system in a way that makes sustained intensity more manageable with time/repetition.

exercises.name: KB Lateral Shift with hand switch

days.cardio_note: Hard efforts sandwiched between moderate segments keep the body working aerobically even at rest, building toward a Max effort at the end. Remind members to protect the Moderate — it's what fuels the Max.

Three rounds of Moderate-to-Hard intervals raise the metabolic baseline before a Max effort finish. This forces the cardiovascular system to recruit efficiently under stress — the adaptation that makes hard efforts feel more manageable.

A calorie target trains members to find their most efficient output — push too little and the interval drags, push too hard and recovery feels short. That pacing awareness is a core driver of cardiovascular adaptation.

Four rounds of incomplete recovery – Moderate rest isn't full recovery by design.  Sustained aerobic stress forces the heart and muscles to become more efficient at clearing fatigure, which is where endurance gains are made.

The descending then re-ascending structure means the body never fully recovers, arriving at Max effort already taxed. That accumulated fatigue is the stimulus that builds real aerobic capacity and improves performance under stress.

A distance target makes output measurable and repeatable — coaches should encourage members to hit consistent splits each round rather than going all out early. Fading splits are a sign of poor pacing, steady splits are a sign of improving aerobic efficiency.

exercises.name: DB Row Neutral Grip on Bench
Ball Rainbow Slam in Iso Split Squat

days.cardio_note: The moderate 2:00 is active work, not rest. The 1:00 hard effort pushes past where the body can sustain balance between effort and recovery, raising that ceiling over time. Consistent splits across all three rounds are the goal.

Each round steps up intentionally — easy, moderate, then hard. The first two intervals set up the 1:30, not slow it down. Consistent hard efforts across all three rounds are the measure of good pacing.
The 15-cal target gives members something to chase each round. Coaches should cue them to note their time and aim to repeat it. The 2:00 moderate is what fuels the next effort.

The short :45 easy intervals allow partial recovery without fully clearing the metabolic demand of the hard effort. This sustained accumulation is what drives cardiovascular adaptation. Coaches should cue members to commit fully to each Hard effort.

At 2:00, the hard effort is long enough that pacing matters — going out too hard compounds fatigue quickly. The 2:00 moderate maintains aerobic engagement without full recovery. Consistent hard splits across both rounds are the goal.

exercises.name: DB Chest Fly to Chest Press

days.cardio_note: Cue members to note their time on the first .3mi and aim to repeat it. The 1:00 easy is intentionally short — consistent splits across rounds mean the aerobic system is keeping up with demand.

Each round builds intentionally — Moderate into Hard into Max — training the body to produce power under accumulated fatigue. Cue members how to correctly pace Moderate/Hard.  The build to Max only works if each interval is distinct.

exercises.name: DB Reverse Lunge with Asymmetrical Load
Res. Band Split Stance Row Alt.

days.cardio_note: The longer hard interval is the stimulus.  The :45 moderate primes and the :45 easy is just enough recovery to repeat quality effort. Cue members that the 1:30 hard should feel consistently challenging across all three rounds.

The 2:00 moderate builds aerobic base before members chase calories, making the hard effort more productive. Calorie-based intervals self-regulate intensity — harder effort, faster finish. Cue members to note their time on the 15 cals and aim to repeat it each round.

Moderate intervals keep heart rate elevated going into each 2:00 hard, maximizing time at high aerobic output, building aerobic capacity. The 1:00 easy allows just enough recovery to repeat quality effort. Cue members to match their pace on both 2:00 hard efforts.

Equal work intervals with a building intensity increase cardiovascular demand with each round. The :45 easy is intentionally short — just enough to repeat the effort. Cue members that each interval must be distinct; resist going too hard too early.

exercises.name: DB Chest Press x2 to Pullover

days.cardio_note: Cue members to note their time on the first .3mi and aim to repeat it. Consistent splits across rounds mean the aerobic system is keeping up with demand.

exercises.name: DB SL Hip Thrust on Bench

days.cardio_note: Equal work-to-rest ratio keeps the heart rate in a sustained aerobic zone, improving cardiac output and the body's ability to clear lactate during the recovery interval — making each subsequent hard effort more efficient over time.
The 2:00 moderate primes the aerobic system going into each hard effort, maximizing time at high aerobic output. Cue members to match their pace on both 2:00 hard efforts.
Three rounds of 2:00 hard with limited recovery accumulates significant aerobic volume — the fatigue is the adaptation. Cue members to hold the same pace across all three rounds.

Starting easy and progressing to moderate before the hard effort ensures the cardiovascular system is fully primed when the 2:00 hard begins. Cue members that both hard efforts should feel equally challenging.

Nine minutes of sustained aerobic work with no true rest — the Moderate intervals keep heart rate elevated while allowing just enough relief to maintain quality through the Hard effort. Cue members that the second moderate is not a cooldown; keep the effort consistent.
